original_abstract:
Experts project that within the next five years, over 90% of digital content will be partially or entirely generated by artificial intelligence. As we transition into this "synthetic society," the boundary between reality and simulation threatens to dissolve entirely. This open-access volume explores four pivotal technologies driving this shift: humanoid robotics, deepfakes, augmented reality, and virtual reality.

Despite being in their formative stages, these technologies are already capable of producing content that is virtually indistinguishable from the real world. The arrival of this synthetic era promises an unprecedented impact on the foundational pillars of modern life, from the integrity of the judicial system and the press to the health of democracy and personal relationships.

Bart van der Sloot examines the technical architecture of these innovations, mapping their professional utility in education, healthcare, entertainment, and finance. However, the book equally addresses their darker potential for fraud, identity theft, and exploitation. Beyond individual harms, the author analyzes the broader systemic risks, including the erosion of individual autonomy, the privatization of the public sphere, and the fracture of societal trust in a post-truth landscape.

The book concludes with a critical evaluation of how the current European legal framework—encompassing privacy, data protection, freedom of expression, and AI regulation—addresses these emerging threats. By identifying significant regulatory gaps, Van der Sloot argues that there are no simple solutions to the challenges posed by our increasingly synthetic future.
humanized_abstract:
by Neil MacCormick On 1 January 2026 artificial intelligence will generate over 90% of all digital information created by humans and what we will be living in is called the synthetic society in which the boundaries that define our relationship with human created, artificial environments will have lost all traces of the distinction between the two. This volume presents developments in four key technologies that are building this world of augmented and virtual reality which includes Deepfakes, Humanoid Robots, Augmented Reality and Virtual Reality.

These are very early days in the development of these technologies, but they have already given rise to holographic content that is highly indistinguishable from reality. The synthetic world that is opening up before us will have a huge effect on the cornerstones of our society – justice, media, democracy and social relations.

The engineer Bart van der Sloot describes the technology behind these inventions and explores their applications in the field of education, medicine, leisure and finance. He also deals with the less pleasant applications of these developments, such as scams, identity theft and other forms of abuse. In addition, he reflects on the social risks associated with them, including the loss of individual freedom, the invasion of the public domain by commercial interests and the increasing fragmentation of society into separate social bubbles. All these are symptoms of a society in which we can no longer agree on what is true and what is not.

The book closes with a critical assessment of how current European law on privacy, data protection, freedom of expression and AI regulation deals with these new challenges. And by summarizing the most significant regulatory shortcomings, Van der Sloot comes to the conclusion that there is no straightforward way to deal with the challenges of our synthetic future.
